vocabulary.meaning_english: to play, to have a good time

examples.japanese_sentence: {週末|しゅうまつ}は{友達|ともだち}と{遊|あそ}びました。
examples.english_translation: I played with my friends over the weekend.
examples.grammar_explanation: The past polite form ～ました is used to describe a completed action in the past.

examples.japanese_sentence: どこで{遊|あそ}ぼうか？
examples.english_translation: Where shall we hang out?
examples.grammar_explanation: The casual volitional form (ou-form) ～ぼう expresses a suggestion or invitation.

examples.japanese_sentence: {海|うみ}で1{時間|じかん}{泳|およ}ぎました。
examples.english_translation: I swam in the sea for one hour.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle で marks the location where the action of swimming takes place.

examples.japanese_sentence: {泳|およ}ぐのが{好|す}きです。
examples.english_translation: I like swimming.
examples.grammar_explanation: The nominalizer の (no) turns the verb 'swim' into a noun phrase to function as the object of 'like'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {空港|くうこう}まで{父|ちち}を{迎|むか}えに{行|い}きます。
examples.english_translation: I will go to the airport to meet my father.
examples.grammar_explanation: The structure 'Verb stem + に行きます' expresses the purpose of going somewhere.

examples.japanese_sentence: {笑顔|えがお}で{客|きゃく}を{迎|むか}えてください。
examples.english_translation: Please welcome the guests with a smile.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～てください is a polite request used with the te-form of the verb.

examples.japanese_sentence: {仕事|しごと}が{多|おお}くて{疲|つか}れました。
examples.english_translation: There was a lot of work, so I got tired.
examples.grammar_explanation: The te-form (ookute) here functions as a conjunction meaning 'because' or 'so'.

examples.japanese_sentence: あまり{疲|つか}れていません。
examples.english_translation: I'm not very tired.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ていません is the negative form of the continuous state, describing the current condition.

examples.japanese_sentence: カバンから{財布|さいふ}を{出|だ}しました。
examples.english_translation: I took my wallet out of the bag.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle から indicates the starting point or source from which something is removed.

examples.japanese_sentence: {宿題|しゅくだい}を{出|だ}してね。
examples.english_translation: Hand in your homework, okay?
examples.grammar_explanation: The te-form (dashite) followed by the particle 'ne' acts as a gentle, casual command.

examples.japanese_sentence: {寒|さむ}いから{店|みせ}に{入|はい}りましょう。
examples.english_translation: It's cold, so let's go into the shop.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ましょう is the polite volitional form used to make a suggestion to the listener.

examples.japanese_sentence: {店|みせ}に{入|はい}ったとき、誰もいませんでした。
examples.english_translation: When I entered the shop, there was no one there.
examples.grammar_explanation: The 'Verb past-plain + とき' structure means 'when (action occurred)'.

examples.japanese_sentence: 8{時|じ}に{店|みせ}を{出|で}ました。
examples.english_translation: I left the shop at 8 o'clock.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle を marks the point of departure when using verbs of leaving like 出ます.

examples.japanese_sentence: {店|みせ}を{出|で}てから、{雨|あめ}が{降|ふ}り{始|はじ}めました。
examples.english_translation: After leaving the shop, it started to rain.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～てから indicates that the second action happens immediately after the first action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {来年|らいねん}、{彼女|かのじょ}と{結婚|けっこん}します。
examples.english_translation: I will marry my girlfriend next year.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle と is used to mean 'with' or 'to' (a partner) for marriage.

examples.japanese_sentence: {兄|あに}はもう{結婚|けっこん}しています。
examples.english_translation: My brother is already married.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～しています indicates a continuous state resulting from a past action (being married).

examples.japanese_sentence: {昨日|きのう}、デパートで{買|か}い{物|もの}しました。
examples.english_translation: I went shopping at the department store yesterday.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle で indicates the location where the activity (shopping) took place.

examples.japanese_sentence: {一緒|いっしょ}に{買|か}い{物|もの}しない？
examples.english_translation: Want to go shopping together?
examples.grammar_explanation: The negative plain form (shinai) with rising intonation acts as a casual invitation.

examples.japanese_sentence: {家族|かぞく}と{食事|しょくじ}しました。
examples.english_translation: I had a meal with my family.
examples.grammar_explanation: Standard polite past tense describing a social action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {静|しず}かなレストランで{食事|しょくじ}したいです。
examples.english_translation: I want to dine at a quiet restaurant.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～たい indicates the speaker's desire when attached to the verb stem.

examples.japanese_sentence: {毎日|まいにち}{公園|こうえん}を{散歩|さんぽ}します。
examples.english_translation: I take a walk in the park every day.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle を is used with verbs of motion (like walk) to mark the space being moved through.

examples.japanese_sentence: {犬|いぬ}と{散歩|さんぽ}するのは{楽|たの}しいです。
examples.english_translation: Walking with my dog is fun.
examples.grammar_explanation: The nominalizer のは turns the phrase into a subject for the adjective 'fun'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {仕事|しごと}が{大変|たいへん}ですが、{頑張|がんば}ります。
examples.english_translation: The work is tough, but I will do my best.
examples.grammar_explanation: ですが (desuga) is a polite conjunction used to connect two contrasting clauses.

examples.japanese_sentence: それは{大変|たいへん}だね。
examples.english_translation: That's tough/awful, isn't it?
examples.grammar_explanation: The casual copula だ with particle ね expresses sympathy for the listener's situation.

examples.japanese_sentence: {新|あたら}しいパソコンが{欲|ほ}しいです。
examples.english_translation: I want a new computer.
examples.grammar_explanation: The object of the adjective 欲しい is marked with the particle が.

examples.japanese_sentence: {何|なに}が{一番|いちばん}{欲|ほ}しい？
examples.english_translation: What do you want most?
examples.grammar_explanation: Ichiban (number one) is used here as an adverb to mean 'the most' or 'best'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {一人|ひとり}だと{寂|さび}しいです。
examples.english_translation: I feel lonely when I'm alone.
examples.grammar_explanation: The conditional particle だと indicates a general situation or predictable result.

examples.japanese_sentence: {寂|さび}しかったから、{友達|ともだち}に{電話|でんわ}した。
examples.english_translation: I was lonely, so I called my friend.
examples.grammar_explanation: The past-casual form (sabishikatta) followed by 'kara' indicates a reason.

examples.japanese_sentence: この{部屋|へや}はとても{広|ひろ}いです。
examples.english_translation: This room is very spacious.
examples.grammar_explanation: Adjectives ending in い are simply followed by です in polite present sentences.

examples.japanese_sentence: もっと{広|ひろ}い{家|いえ}に{住|す}みたい。
examples.english_translation: I want to live in a wider/bigger house.
examples.grammar_explanation: The adjective directly precedes the noun it modifies.

examples.japanese_sentence: {道|みち}が{狭|せま}いので、{気|き}をつけてください。
examples.english_translation: The road is narrow, so please be careful.
examples.grammar_explanation: ので is a formal way to express cause or reason.

examples.japanese_sentence: この{寮|りょう}は{狭|せま}すぎる。
examples.english_translation: This dormitory is too small.
examples.grammar_explanation: Adding ～すぎる to the adjective stem expresses an excessive degree.

examples.japanese_sentence: {市役所|しやくしょ}で{手続|てつづ}きをします。
examples.english_translation: I will do the paperwork at the city hall.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle で marks the location of the action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {市役所|しやくしょ}へどうやって{行|い}きますか。
examples.english_translation: How do I get to the city hall?
examples.grammar_explanation: どうやって is an interrogative phrase used to ask for a method or directions.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明日|あした}プールに{行|い}きませんか。
examples.english_translation: Won't you go to the pool tomorrow?
examples.grammar_explanation: The negative-question form ～ませんか is a polite way to invite someone.

examples.japanese_sentence: このプールは{深|ふか}くない。
examples.english_translation: This pool is not deep.
examples.grammar_explanation: The negative form of い-adjectives is created by replacing い with くない.

examples.japanese_sentence: {川|かわ}の{近|ちか}くを{走|はし}りました。
examples.english_translation: I ran near the river.
examples.grammar_explanation: The structure 'Noun + の近く' acts as a locational noun phrase.

examples.japanese_sentence: {川|かわ}で{泳|およ}ぐのは{危険|きけん}だよ。
examples.english_translation: Swimming in the river is dangerous, you know.
examples.grammar_explanation: The sentence-ending particle だよ adds an advisory or assertive tone.

examples.japanese_sentence: {大学|だいがく}で{経済|けいざい}を{勉強|べんきょう}しています。
examples.english_translation: I am studying economics at university.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ています is used here to describe a current, long-term activity like majoring in a subject.

examples.japanese_sentence: {日本|にほん}の{経済|けいざい}について{教|おし}えてください。
examples.english_translation: Please tell me about the Japanese economy.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～について is a compound particle meaning 'about' or 'concerning'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {美術|びじゅつ}の{時間|じかん}は10{時|じ}からです。
examples.english_translation: Art class starts from 10 o'clock.
examples.grammar_explanation: から marks the starting time of an event.

examples.japanese_sentence: {美術|びじゅつ}には{興味|きょうみ}がないんだ。
examples.english_translation: I'm not interested in fine arts.
examples.grammar_explanation: The explanatory んだ provides context or emphasis to the statement.

examples.japanese_sentence: {趣味|しゅみ}は{釣|つ}りです。
examples.english_translation: My hobby is fishing.
examples.grammar_explanation: A simple 'A は B です' identification sentence.

examples.japanese_sentence: {父|ちち}と{川|かわ}へ{釣|つ}りに{行|い}きました。
examples.english_translation: I went fishing to the river with my father.
examples.grammar_explanation: Here, 釣り functions as the purpose noun in the 'Place + へ + Purpose + に行きます' structure.

examples.japanese_sentence: {日曜日|にちようび}に{海|うみ}で{釣|つ}りをします。
examples.english_translation: I will fish in the sea on Sunday.
examples.grammar_explanation: The verb します turns the noun 釣り into an active verb phrase.

examples.japanese_sentence: {一緒|いっしょ}に{釣|つ}りをしませんか。
examples.english_translation: Won't you fish with me?
examples.grammar_explanation: ～しませんか is the polite invitation form of the irregular verb する.

examples.japanese_sentence: {冬|ふゆ}はスキーが{一番|いちばん}です。
examples.english_translation: Skiing is the best in winter.
examples.grammar_explanation: Using 'Noun + が一番です' indicates that something is the best or superior.

examples.japanese_sentence: スキーが{上手|じょうず}になりたい。
examples.english_translation: I want to become good at skiing.
examples.grammar_explanation: Adjective stem + になる expresses a change in state or ability.

examples.japanese_sentence: {北海道|ほっかいどう}でスキーをしました。
examples.english_translation: I skied in Hokkaido.
examples.grammar_explanation: The past polite form ～ました indicates the action was completed.

examples.japanese_sentence: スキーをしたことがありますか。
examples.english_translation: Have you ever skied?
examples.grammar_explanation: ～たことがあります is used to ask about past experiences.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今|いま}、{会議|かいぎ}の{準備|じゅんび}をしています。
examples.english_translation: I am preparing for the meeting now.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle の links the two nouns 'meeting' and 'preparation'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {会議|かいぎ}はもう{終|お}わったよ。
examples.english_translation: The meeting is already over.
examples.grammar_explanation: もう (mou) means 'already' when used with a past tense verb.

examples.japanese_sentence: 3{時|じ}から{会議|かいぎ}をします。
examples.english_translation: We will hold a meeting from 3 o'clock.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle を indicates the object of the action 'to do'.

examples.japanese_sentence: どこで{会議|かいぎ}をするの？
examples.english_translation: Where are you holding the meeting?
examples.grammar_explanation: The sentence-ending particle の creates a casual, seeking-information question.

examples.japanese_sentence: {登録|とうろく}には{身分|みぶん}{証明|しょうめい}{書|しょ}が{必要|ひつよう}です。
examples.english_translation: Identification is required for registration.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle には (ni wa) sets 'for the purpose of registration' as the topic.

examples.japanese_sentence: これは{無料|むりょう}の{登録|とうろく}ですか。
examples.english_translation: Is this a free registration?
examples.grammar_explanation: A basic noun-modifying structure using の.

examples.japanese_sentence: {住所|じゅうしょ}を{登録|とうろく}しました。
examples.english_translation: I registered my address.
examples.grammar_explanation: The verb します is combined with the noun 登録 to create the action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {名前|なまえ}を{登録|とうろく}しておいてください。
examples.english_translation: Please register your name in advance.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ておく indicates doing an action in preparation for the future.

examples.japanese_sentence: {週末|しゅうまつ}は何をしますか。
examples.english_translation: What will you do on the weekend?
examples.grammar_explanation: The topic particle は indicates the timeframe being discussed.

examples.japanese_sentence: {週末|しゅうまつ}はゆっくりしたい。
examples.english_translation: I want to relax this weekend.
examples.grammar_explanation: The adverb 'yukkuri' (slowly/at ease) modifies the desire 'shitai'.

examples.japanese_sentence: 7{時|じ}ごろ{晩|ばん}ご{飯|はん}を{食|た}べます。
examples.english_translation: I eat dinner at about 7 o'clock.
examples.grammar_explanation: ごろ is a suffix used specifically with time points to indicate approximation.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明日|あした}の{昼|ひる}ごろ{会|あ}える？
examples.english_translation: Can we meet around noon tomorrow?
examples.grammar_explanation: The potential form 'aeru' (can meet) is used in a casual question.

examples.japanese_sentence: {何|なに}か{飲|の}みませんか。
examples.english_translation: Would you like something to drink?
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle か attached to an interrogative makes it indefinite (something).

examples.japanese_sentence: {何|なに}かいい{事|こと}あった？
examples.english_translation: Did something good happen?
examples.grammar_explanation: In casual speech, particles like が (ga) are often omitted.

examples.japanese_sentence: {夏|なつ}{休|やす}みにどこかへ{行|い}きたいです。
examples.english_translation: I want to go somewhere during summer vacation.
examples.grammar_explanation: The directional particle へ follows どこか to indicate destination.

examples.japanese_sentence: どこかで{財布|さいふ}を{落|お}としたかもしれない。
examples.english_translation: I might have dropped my wallet somewhere.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～かもしれない expresses a possibility or conjecture ('might').

examples.japanese_sentence: お{腹|なか}が{空|す}いたので、ピザを{食|た}べましょう。
examples.english_translation: I'm hungry, so let's eat pizza.
examples.grammar_explanation: The casual past form (suita) plus 'node' indicates the reason for the suggestion.

examples.japanese_sentence: お{腹|なか}が{空|す}きましたか。
examples.english_translation: Are you hungry?
examples.grammar_explanation: Hunger in Japanese is expressed as a completed past action ('stomach became empty').

vocabulary.reading_furigana: おなかがいっぱいです

examples.japanese_sentence: もうお{腹|なか}がいっぱいです。
examples.english_translation: I am already full.
examples.grammar_explanation: もう (already) emphasizes the state of completion.

examples.japanese_sentence: お{腹|なか}がいっぱいだから、もう{食|た}べられない。
examples.english_translation: I'm full, so I can't eat anymore.
examples.grammar_explanation: The potential negative 'taberarenai' expresses inability to eat.

examples.japanese_sentence: のどが{渇|かわ}いたから、お{茶|ちゃ}が{飲|の}みたい。
examples.english_translation: I'm thirsty, so I want to drink tea.
examples.grammar_explanation: Like hunger, thirst is expressed as a past-tense realization of a state.

examples.japanese_sentence: のどが{渇|かわ}きましたね。ジュースを{買|か}いましょうか。
examples.english_translation: You're thirsty, aren't you? Shall I buy some juice?
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ましょうか is used to offer assistance to the listener.

examples.japanese_sentence: 「いい{天気|てんき}ですね」「そうですね」
examples.english_translation: 'Nice weather, isn't it?' 'Yes, it is.'
examples.grammar_explanation: そうですね is used to express agreement or sympathy with the speaker's comment.

examples.japanese_sentence: そうですね、ちょっと{考|かんが}えさせてください。
examples.english_translation: Well, let me think about it for a moment.
examples.grammar_explanation: It can also be used as a filler or a way to pause before answering.

examples.japanese_sentence: 「タクシーで{行|い}きませんか」「そうしましょう」
examples.english_translation: 'Why don't we go by taxi?' 'Let's do that.'
examples.grammar_explanation: This phrase is used to agree with a suggestion or proposal made by another person.

examples.japanese_sentence: あ、いいですね。そうしましょう！
examples.english_translation: Ah, that's a good idea. Let's do that!
examples.grammar_explanation: Adding exclamation marks or rising intonation shows enthusiastic agreement.

examples.japanese_sentence: {店員|てんいん}：ご{注文|ちゅうもん}は{決|き}まりましたか。
examples.english_translation: Clerk: Have you decided on your order?
examples.grammar_explanation: The prefix 'go' is an honorific attached to 'chuumon' (order) to show respect to the customer.

examples.japanese_sentence: ご{注文|ちゅうもん}は、お{揃|そろ}いですか。
examples.english_translation: Is that everyone's order? (lit. Is the order complete?)
examples.grammar_explanation: A common phrase used by waiters to confirm if anything else is needed.

examples.japanese_sentence: この{魚|さかな}{定食|ていしょく}を{一|ひと}つください。
examples.english_translation: One of this fish set meal, please.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～をください is a standard polite way to order items in a restaurant.

examples.japanese_sentence: {定食|ていしょく}はごはんとお{代|か}わりができます。
examples.english_translation: With the set meal, you can get free refills of rice.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ができる expresses possibility or permission.

vocabulary.meaning_english: bowl of rice topped with beef

examples.japanese_sentence: {昼|ひる}ご{飯|はん}に{牛丼|ぎゅうどん}を{食|た}べました。
examples.english_translation: I ate a beef bowl for lunch.
examples.grammar_explanation: Standard polite past tense describing a past event.

examples.japanese_sentence: {牛丼|ぎゅうどん}、{並|なみ}でいい？
examples.english_translation: Is a regular-sized beef bowl okay?
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'de' here signifies the choice or condition 'at' or 'as'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {少々|しょうしょう}お{待|ま}ちください。
examples.english_translation: Please wait for a short moment.
examples.grammar_explanation: This is an honorific request form: 'O + Verb stem + kudasai'.

examples.japanese_sentence: そのままお{待|ま}ちください。
examples.english_translation: Please wait just as you are / Please stay on the line.
examples.grammar_explanation: そのまま indicates maintaining the current state or position.

examples.japanese_sentence: お{会計|かいけい}は{別々|べつべつ}にお{願|ねが}いします。
examples.english_translation: Separate checks, please.
examples.grammar_explanation: 別々に is an adverb used to describe doing an action individually.

examples.japanese_sentence: {袋|ふくろ}に{別々|べつべつ}に{入|い}れて。
examples.english_translation: Put them in the bags separately.
examples.grammar_explanation: A casual request using the te-form (irete).

examples.japanese_sentence: ロシアはとても{広|ひろ}い{国|くに}です。
examples.english_translation: Russia is a very large country.
examples.grammar_explanation: An i-adjective modifying a noun in a polite sentence.

examples.japanese_sentence: ロシアから{来|き}た{友達|ともだち}がいます。
examples.english_translation: I have a friend who came from Russia.
examples.grammar_explanation: The relative clause 'Russia kara kita' modifies the noun 'tomodachi'.
